Common EPF claim rejection reasons and resolutions compiled from EPFO field office reports and member portal data. TOP 10 CLAIM REJECTION REASONS: 1. BANK ACCOUNT NOT VERIFIED / IFSC INCORRECT Fix: Re-enter bank details in KYC section. Get current IFSC from bank. Wait for employer approval. 2. DATE OF EXIT NOT UPDATED Fix: If 60+ days since leaving — self-update via Manage > Mark Exit. If less than 60 days — ask employer to update in their portal. 3. NAME MISMATCH (Aadhaar vs PF records) Fix: Submit Joint Declaration for name correction. Employer approves. Then re-link Aadhaar. 4. MEMBER ALREADY SETTLED (Duplicate claim attempt) Fix: Check passbook — if first claim was settled, the amount should be in your bank. If not, file grievance. 5. UAN NOT AADHAAR SEEDED Fix: Link Aadhaar via member portal > Manage > KYC > Aadhaar. Wait for UIDAI verification. 6. CLAIM FOR WRONG PURPOSE (e.g. Form 19 filed while still employed) Fix: Wait 2 months after leaving service, then file again. 7. INSUFFICIENT SERVICE FOR ADVANCE TYPE Fix: Check eligibility table. Select correct advance purpose matching your service period. 8. DOJ (Date of Joining) NOT UPDATED OR INCORRECT Fix: Contact employer to update DOJ via employer portal. Raise JD request if employer is unavail

Practical guide on TDS (Tax Deducted at Source) for PF withdrawals, reflecting rules effective from April 2022 and updated in 2024. THE SIMPLE RULE — TWO CONDITIONS MUST BOTH BE TRUE FOR TDS: Condition 1: Total service is LESS than 5 years (counting service across all employers if PF was transferred) AND Condition 2: Withdrawal amount is MORE than Rs.50,000 If EITHER condition is false → NO TDS is deducted. COUNTING THE 5 YEARS: - Service includes all employers where PF was contributed AND transferred to your current account - Example: 3 years at Company A (transferred) + 2.5 years at Company B = 5.5 years total → NO TDS - If you did NOT transfer PF from previous employer, each account is treated separately - TDS applies per withdrawal claim, not per employer TDS RATES (for residential Indians with PAN): Scenario | TDS Rate PAN valid + service < 5 yrs + > 50k | 10% No valid PAN + service < 5 yrs + >50k | 20% Service 5+ years (regardless of amount)| 0% Amount ≤ Rs.50,000 | 0% HOW TO SUBMIT FORM 15G (to get zero TDS): WHO: Resident individual below 60 years whose estimated total income for the year is below Rs.2,50,000 (i.e., below basic exemption limit). WHEN: Submit BEFORE filing the withdrawal claim. Cannot be submitted after TDS is deducted. WHERE (Online): Login t

[Source 1] doc_id=INOPERATIVE_ACCOUNTS | date=2024-01-01 | domain=pf
An EPF account is classified as Inoperative when no contribution has been received for 3 years after:
        - Retirement
        - Permanent migration abroad
        - Death
        
        INTEREST ON INOPERATIVE ACCOUNTS:
        - At present, all accounts earn interest up to age 58 of the member
        - After account becomes inoperative, no further interest is credited
        - Account becomes inoperative after 36 months from date of retirement (55 years+) or permanent migration
        
        WHAT TO DO WITH INOPERATIVE ACCOUNT:
        - If still employed in covered establishment: Get amount transferred to new account (online or offline Form 13)
        - If retired: Withdraw the amount by submitting withdrawal claim
        
        UNCLAIMED PF (SENIOR CITIZENS FUND):
        Unclaimed EPF amounts are transferred to Senior Citizens Welfare Fund (SCWF) after 7 years of becoming inoperative. However, members can still claim this amount from EPFO.
        
        HOW TO CLAIM INOPERATIVE ACCOUNT:
        - Trace the account using establishment name or PF number
        - Contact the employer for details
        - Submit withdrawal claim to concerned Regional PF Office with proper KYC documentation
        
        PASSBOOK FOR INOPERATIVE ACCOUNTS:

Under the Minimum Wages Act 1948, the state government of Punjab sets the statutory minimum wage for scheduled employments. As of the 2024 notification, an unskilled worker must receive at least ₹11500 per month. A skilled or highly skilled worker must receive at least ₹14000 per month, excluding discretionary allowances. Paying below these rates violates Section 22 of the Minimum Wages Act and is a criminal offence.

Gratuity can also be wholly forfeited if the termination is due to an offence involving moral turpitude. Under Section 4 of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity is payable to an employee on termination of service after completing a minimum of 5 years of continuous service. The 5-year rule is waived in cases of death or disability. Calculation formula: (Last Drawn Monthly Salary × 15 × Years of Service) ÷ 26. Under Section 4(6) of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity can be forfeited wholly or partially if an employee's services are terminated due to: (a) willful omission or negligence causing damage or loss to the employer's property; or (b) riotous or disorderly conduct or any other act of violence. Termination for ordinary misconduct (e.g., poor performance, insubordination without violence) does NOT forfeit gratuity — the employee remains entitled to gratuity if they have completed 5 years of service. The employer must follow a disciplinary inquiry and issue a charge sheet before forfeiting gratuity.

USER QUERY:
Worked 5 years at factory company. retirement. Last salary ₹35000. Gratuity?

DOMAIN: labour

RETRIEVED PASSAGES:
[Source 1] doc_id=TERMINATION_MISCONDUCT_GRATUITY | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under Section 4(6) of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity can be forfeited wholly or partially if an employee's services are terminated due to: (a) willful omission or negligence causing damage or loss to the employer's property; or (b) riotous or disorderly conduct or any other act of violence. Gratuity can also be wholly forfeited if the termination is due to an offence involving moral turpitude. Termination for ordinary misconduct (e.g., poor performance, insubordination without violence) does NOT forfeit gratuity — the employee remains entitled to gratuity if they have completed 5 years of service. The employer must follow a disciplinary inquiry and issue a charge sheet before forfeiting gratuity.

---

[Source 2] doc_id=GRATUITY_ACT_S4_ELIG | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under Section 4 of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity is payable to an employee on termination of service after completing a minimum of 5 years of continuous service. The 5-year rule is waived in cases of death or disability. Calculation formula: (Last Drawn Monthly Salary × 15 × Years of Service) ÷ 26. 'Last drawn salary' means basic wages plus dearness allowance. The maximum gratuity payable is ₹20 Lakhs. Gratuity is payable within 30 days of becoming due; the employer must pay simple interest at 10% per annum for any delay.

---

[Source 3] doc_id=GRATUITY_COURT_RULING_4Y8M | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
The Madras High Court has held that an employee who completes 4 years and 240 days (approximately 4 years and 8 months) of service qualifies for gratuity, as 240 days constitutes a full year of service for establishments operating 6 days per week. For 5-day-week establishments, the threshold is 190 days in the fifth year. This judicial interpretation is widely followed and effectively reduces the practical threshold from 5 full calendar years.



SLOTS FILLED:
{
  "intent": "gratuity",
  "employment_years": 5,
  "termination_reason": "retirement",
  "employer_type": "factory",
  "last_drawn_salary": 35000
}

**Reasoning [TERMINATION_MISCONDUCT_GRATUITY]:**
- Continuous service of 5 years meets the 5-year minimum
- Retirement is a qualifying termination reason

**Calculation [TERMINATION_MISCONDUCT_GRATUITY]:**
Gratuity = (Last salary × 15 × years) / 26
        = (₹35,000 × 15 × 5) / 26
        = ₹100,961

1. Submit Form I to employer within 30 days [TERMINATION_MISCONDUCT_GRATUITY].

full_prompt:
USER QUERY:
Worked 5 years at factory company. retirement. Last salary ₹25000. Gratuity?

DOMAIN: labour

RETRIEVED PASSAGES:
[Source 1] doc_id=GRATUITY_ACT_S4_ELIG | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under Section 4 of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity is payable to an employee on termination of service after completing a minimum of 5 years of continuous service. The 5-year rule is waived in cases of death or disability. Calculation formula: (Last Drawn Monthly Salary × 15 × Years of Service) ÷ 26. 'Last drawn salary' means basic wages plus dearness allowance. The maximum gratuity payable is ₹20 Lakhs. Gratuity is payable within 30 days of becoming due; the employer must pay simple interest at 10% per annum for any delay.

---

[Source 2] doc_id=TERMINATION_MISCONDUCT_GRATUITY | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under Section 4(6) of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity can be forfeited wholly or partially if an employee's services are terminated due to: (a) willful omission or negligence causing damage or loss to the employer's property; or (b) riotous or disorderly conduct or any other act of violence. Gratuity can also be wholly forfeited if the termination is due to an offence involving moral turpitude. Termination for ordinary misconduct (e.g., poor performance, insubordination without violence) does NOT forfeit gratuity — the employee remains entitled to gratuity if they have completed 5 years of service. The employer must follow a disciplinary inquiry and issue a charge sheet before forfeiting gratuity.

---

[Source 3] doc_id=GRATUITY_COURT_RULING_4Y8M | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
The Madras High Court has held that an employee who completes 4 years and 240 days (approximately 4 years and 8 months) of service qualifies for gratuity, as 240 days constitutes a full year of service for establishments operating 6 days per week. For 5-day-week establishments, the threshold is 190 days in the fifth year. This judicial interpretation is widely followed and effectively reduces the practical threshold from 5 full calendar years.



SLOTS FILLED:
{
  "intent": "gratuity",
  "employment_years": 5,
  "termination_reason": "retirement",
  "employer_type": "factory",
  "last_drawn_salary": 25000
}

Produce the final answer now. IMPORTANT: every factual claim must include [DOC_ID] in brackets — no exceptions. If you cannot cite, omit the claim.
chosen:
**Result:** Eligible for gratuity [GRATUITY_ACT_S4_ELIG].

**Reasoning [GRATUITY_ACT_S4_ELIG]:**
- Continuous service of 5 years meets the 5-year minimum
- Retirement is a qualifying termination reason

**Calculation [GRATUITY_ACT_S4_ELIG]:**
Gratuity = (Last salary × 15 × years) / 26
        = (₹25,000 × 15 × 5) / 26
        = ₹72,115

**Next Steps:**
1. Submit Form I to employer within 30 days [GRATUITY_ACT_S4_ELIG].
2. Employer must pay within 30 days.
3. If unpaid, file with Controlling Authority.

- Retirement is a qualifying termination reason

**Calculation [TERMINATION_MISCONDUCT_GRATUITY]:**

        = ₹72,115

Under Section 4 of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity is payable to an employee on termination of service after completing a minimum of 5 years of continuous service. Gratuity is payable within 30 days of becoming due; the employer must pay simple interest at 10% per annum for any delay.

---

[Source 2] doc_id=TERMINATION_MISCONDUCT_GRATUITY | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under Section 4(6) of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity can be forfeited wholly or partially if an employee's services are terminated due to: (a) willful omission or negligence causing damage or loss to the employer's property; or (b) riotous or disorderly conduct or any other act of violence. Gratuity can also be wholly forfeited if the termination is due to an offence involving moral turpitude. Termination for ordinary misconduct (e.g., poor performance, insubordination without violence) does NOT forfeit gratuity — the employee remains entitled to gratuity if they have completed 5 years of service. full_prompt:
USER QUERY:
Worked 7 years at private company. employer_terminated. Last salary ₹25000. Gratuity?

DOMAIN: labour

RETRIEVED PASSAGES:
[Source 1] doc_id=GRATUITY_ACT_S4_ELIG | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under Section 4 of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity is payable to an employee on termination of service after completing a minimum of 5 years of continuous service. The 5-year rule is waived in cases of death or disability. Calculation formula: (Last Drawn Monthly Salary × 15 × Years of Service) ÷ 26. 'Last drawn salary' means basic wages plus dearness allowance. The maximum gratuity payable is ₹20 Lakhs. Gratuity is payable within 30 days of becoming due; the employer must pay simple interest at 10% per annum for any delay.

---

[Source 2] doc_id=TERMINATION_MISCONDUCT_GRATUITY | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under Section 4(6) of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity can be forfeited wholly or partially if an employee's services are terminated due to: (a) willful omission or negligence causing damage or loss to the employer's property; or (b) riotous or disorderly conduct or any other act of violence. Gratuity can also be wholly forfeited if the termination is due to an offence involving moral turpitude. Termination for ordinary misconduct (e.g., poor performance, insubordination without violence) does NOT forfeit gratuity — the employee remains entitled to gratuity if they have completed 5 years of service. The employer must follow a disciplinary inquiry and issue a charge sheet before forfeiting gratuity.

---

[Source 3] doc_id=GRATUITY_COURT_RULING_4Y8M | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
The Madras High Court has held that an employee who completes 4 years and 240 days (approximately 4 years and 8 months) of service qualifies for gratuity, as 240 days constitutes a full year of service for establishments operating 6 days per week. For 5-day-week establishments, the threshold is 190 days in the fifth year. This judicial interpretation is widely followed and effectively reduces the practical threshold from 5 full calendar years.



SLOTS FILLED:
{
  "intent": "gratuity",
  "employment_years": 7,
  "termination_reason": "employer_terminated",
  "employer_type": "private",
  "last_drawn_salary": 25000
}

Produce the final answer now. IMPORTANT: every factual claim must include [DOC_ID] in brackets — no exceptions. If you cannot cite, omit the claim.
chosen:
**Result:**
You are eligible for gratuity.

**Reason:** You have completed more than 5 years of service (7 years) and your termination reason is employer_terminated.

**Next Steps:**

1. Check with your employer for the gratuity calculation, which is typically 15 days' salary for each completed year of service.
2. Ensure your employer has followed the correct procedure for gratuity payment, including conducting a disciplinary inquiry and issuing a charge sheet if applicable.
3. If you're not receiving gratuity, consider consulting a labour lawyer or filing a complaint with the labour department.

**Warning/Caveat:** Termination due to ordinary misconduct without violence or insubordination does not forfeit gratuity, but termination due to willful omission, negligence, or an offence involving moral turpitude may result in partial or complete forfeiture of gratuity. [TERMINATION_MISCONDUCT_GRATUITY]

Under the Maternity Benefit (Amendment) Act 2017, a female employee is entitled to 26 weeks of paid maternity leave for the first two live births, and 12 weeks for subsequent births. Eligibility requires 80 days of actual work in the 12 months preceding the expected delivery. The benefit applies to all establishments with 10 or more employees. Employers with 50 or more employees must provide creche facilities.

**Result:** You are eligible to contest the gratuity at the Controlling Authority.

**Steps:**

1. Gather all relevant documents related to your employment and gratuity, including proof of service, last drawn salary, and any previous gratuity calculations.
2. Submit your claim to the Controlling Authority, which will review your case and make a decision based on the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972 [GRATUITY_ACT_S4_ELIG].

* If the employer is found to have delayed payment, you may be entitled to simple interest at 10% per annum [GRATUITY_ACT_S4_ELIG].
* The maximum gratuity payable is ₹20 Lakhs, so ensure that your claim does not exceed this amount.

Note: The 5-year rule for continuous service is waived in cases of death or disability [GRATUITY_ACT_S4_ELIG].

An employee who has been wrongfully terminated has several remedies depending on their category: (1) Workmen (as defined under the Industrial Disputes Act) can raise an industrial dispute before the Labour Commissioner, and if unresolved, before the Labour Court, which can award reinstatement with back wages. (2) Managerial or supervisory employees (not 'workmen') must approach civil courts or the High Court; they cannot use Labour Courts. (3) Employees in government or public sector establishments may have access to service tribunals or departmental appeals. Filing a complaint under the ID Act must be done within 3 years of the wrongful termination. If the employer is in an 'essential service', different notice requirements apply.

Under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act 1946, certified standing orders for a factory or establishment must specify the notice period required for termination. For permanent workmen, the typical minimum notice period is 30 days. If the offer letter or contract specifies a longer notice period (e.g., 60 or 90 days), that contractual period applies. An employer who terminates without giving the agreed notice must pay wages in lieu of notice. An employee who resigns without serving notice may have pay deducted for the shortfall.
